annotation.text:
Cultflicks is a SaaS based streaming platform that became popular for streaming unpopular movies.
It had sold subscriptions for a long time but eventually, the growth stalled.
The company wanted to improve its product offering for casual viewers by adding a pay-per-view subscription model.
This would mean that subscribers would only pay for the content that they watched and if they didn't watch anything, they wouldn't be charged for that month.

After a successful advertising campaign, Cultflicks now has customers across two different subscription levels.
Premium subscribers, who pay a standard monthly fee for all content, and pay-per-view subscribers whose monthly charge will vary depending on their consumption each month.

The Cultflicks example stream will provide a batch flow that records viewing statistics, classifies users according to their usage, and processes data into billable information.
This stream highlights:

An actual stream flow from raw data

Data enrichment

User classification to multiple use cases, like billing, notification, settlement and analytics

A step-by-step preview of data transformation from "raw usage data" to data that the business can gain insight from, for example, data related to churn or upselling opportunities.

This example stream is explained in detail here: https://infozone.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/DAZ/pages/7880549/Cultflicks+Video+Streaming.

mappings.expression: "Hi dear " + @{payload.name} + ", Your email is " + @{payload.email} + ", your country is " + @{payload.country} + ", and you were born on " + @{payload.birth_year} + ". " + @{payload.nudge_msg} + " " + @{payload.metrics_msg} + "  Best regards, Cultflicks"

annotation.text:
Reformat the date field.
From: YYYY-MMM-DD
To: YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss.SSS[Z]

Split view events into completed or not completed based on the view percent value calculated in the previous function. 

We use the Route function and its condition rules for this. Based on this classification, we send the completed view events to Settlement, and the not completed ones to Analytics.

Use a Data Aggregator processor to group view events by user and by month. This aggregated data is then used to classify users based on their activity level: light, medium, or heavy usage. We also assess the risk of user churn. Based on these classifications, we generate relevant information and nudging messages, which can be sent to the user or included on their next invoice.

In this example, a Script Aggregator applies simple, rules-based classifications. In a real-world scenario, user classification would likely be performed by an external AI service using a machine learning model, accessed via an HTTP Client processor.

Finally, the data is formatted for billing, and a No-op forwarder is used to simulate sending it to a billing system. In a production environment, this step would typically use a forwarder such as SAP Subscription Billing, Zuora Usage File, or an Email forwarder.

Calculate the view percent per view event record, based on actual measured view duration (from the usage data), and maximum view duration (based on the product master data we looked up previously), so we can see how much of each movie was actually watched, and enrich them with this percentage. We use a Map processor for this, which has a simple formula language that can be used for simple calculations like this one. We then send the enriched view event records to two different places: to billing and to check view completions.

Use Count and Script functions to generate simulated sample usage data representing views of really bad movies. In a real-world scenario, we would have collected this from video streaming server databases, using one or more, e.g., Amazon S3, Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage, HTTP Client, or SFTP collectors. In a real-world scenario, there would have been a validation step after collection to ensure data is correct and clean.

Here, we prepare usage data summaries for settlement purposes. A Data Aggregator processor is used to group completed view events by movie (rather than by user) for each month. This allows downstream systems to calculate the royalties owed to each movie IP owner. The aggregated data is then formatted and, for demonstration purposes, sent to a simulated external system using a No-op forwarder. In a real-world scenario, this data would typically be sent to an ERP system for accounts payable or to a billing system for partner settlements, using the appropriate forwarder.

Simulate looking up the master data from external systems, then use it to enrich the usage data with user and product information. In this case, the product information is data about the movie, and the user information is, among other things, mapping a technical userId to a user email and other information we will need later in the stream. We use the Script processors for this here. In a real-world scenario, we would have used processors, for example, Database query, HTTP Client, Salesforce query, or SAP Subscription Billing query to do these lookups in external systems. In this simple example, we mock this using the Script functions instead.

Here, we prepare usage data summaries for analytics purposes. A Data Aggregator processor is used to group incomplete view events by movie (rather than by user) for each month. This enables Cultflicks to analyze how their content is performing. The aggregated data is then formatted and, for demonstration purposes, sent to a simulated external system using a No-op forwarder. In a real-world scenario, this data would typically be sent to an analytics database using the appropriate forwarder, such as a Database forwarder.

Here we use a Script processor to format an email message to users with a friendly message, some usage statistics, and a nudge message, which we then simulate sending using a No-Op forwarder. In a real-life scenario, this would use an Email Notification forwarder.
